critique.summary: The article reports that Honda is discontinuing its electric vehicle (EV) programs, including halting development of new EV models and stopping production of the Prologue, a GM-built EV. The author argues this decision risks Honda falling behind in the automotive industry's shift toward electric drivetrains and software-defined vehicles, potentially jeopardizing its future competitiveness.
mainPoints: Honda has stopped development of its electric Acura RDX, Honda 0 sedan and SUV, and will cease production of the GM-designed Prologue EV.
The company blames U.S. tariffs and Chinese competition but lacked a strong EV strategy from the start.
Honda risks missing out on key industry shifts toward electric drivetrains and software-defined vehicles (SDVs).
Legacy automakers like Honda often struggle with EVs because they try to adapt internal combustion engine platforms rather than design EVs from the ground up.
Honda's identity as an internal combustion engine and driver-focused car company is challenged by the rise of EVs and autonomous driving technologies.
Honda's competitiveness is declining, especially in China, where it faces losses and strong competition from newer EV manufacturers.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims align with publicly available information as of early 2026, including reports from Automotive News about Honda halting EV projects and discontinuing the Prologue. The challenges faced by legacy automakers in transitioning to EVs and software-defined vehicles are widely documented in industry analysis. Honda's financial struggles and competitive pressures in China are also supported by recent earnings reports. However, the article presents a critical perspective without citing multiple sources or perspectives, relying heavily on analysis and opinion from the author.
considerations: The article does not provide direct statements or responses from Honda beyond referencing their earnings report.
It lacks perspectives from industry analysts, Honda executives, or other stakeholders that might contextualize Honda's strategic decisions.
The financial and strategic implications for Honda's global operations beyond China are not deeply explored.
Potential plans Honda may have for future EV or SDV development beyond the current halted projects are not discussed.
The article does not consider the broader market or regulatory factors influencing Honda's decisions in detail.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Honda's decision to kill its EV programs, with many commenters criticizing the move as a strategic failure amid the industry's shift to electric and software-defined vehicles. Some highlight challenges with EV infrastructure, software updates, and market readiness, while others defend Honda's cautious approach and emphasize the evolving automotive landscape.

comments.author: rkagerer
comments.text:
Honda is setting itself up for failure on the second disruption sweeping the automotive industry: the software-defined vehicle (SDV), which has core capabilities that can be upgraded and improved over time.

No thank you. Not sure why the author frames this as a good thing. They've been bamboozled by the automakers and have got it backwards - you're buying a vehicle that already has the capabilities, but are disabled, then paying rent (or a fee) to turn them on. I'm much more likely to buy from a manufacturer that doesn't play these games.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately identifies the risk Honda faces by ignoring software-defined vehicles, and critiques the monetization model of enabling features post-purchase, which is a valid consumer concern.

comments.author: TrackerFF
comments.text:
I live in a top EV market, Norway.

ICE cars have been planned out for years now, and something like 96% of all new cars in Norway were EV last year.

Basically, if you plan on keeping selling ICE cars, you're removing yourself from the market here. There's no future for new personal ICE cars here.

I figure most other countries will be the same.
comments.analysis: Provides real-world data from Norway, a leading EV market, supporting the claim that ICE vehicles have limited future viability in such markets.

comments.author: pclowes
comments.text:
Everyone is saying EVs are the future but most EVs cannot compete with many of Honda’s offerings.

Eg. I need to move 6 people and significant gear (skiing, camping, biking etc) long remote distances.

There is no EV that can do that really. And the ones that come close are easily $20-30k higher than an Odyssey. Plus the durability of large EVs is far from proven while the 300k mile club of Odyssey owners is large.

I need Suburban/Minivan functionality out of a proven OEM at a competitive price point. (I also need to see my friends with Rivians etc not having to schedule their vacation around charger availability. Have seen this waste hours and hours of time)
comments.analysis: Raises practical concerns about EVs' current limitations in capacity, range, and cost compared to traditional vehicles, reflecting a valid consumer perspective.

comments.author: bryanlarsen
comments.text:
OTOH, it really looks like Toyota is Goldilocks. Most companies invested too much too early and had to write off a substantial amount, but Toyota is rolling into 2027 with a small but nice selection of EV's.

Over 25% of vehicles sold world-wide were electric in 2025, and that percentage is steadily increasing. So VW & Ford were "too hot", Honda is looking like "too cold" and Toyota might be the "just right" of the three bears.
comments.analysis: Offers a balanced view of the EV market, suggesting Toyota's measured approach may be more sustainable than Honda's or others' strategies.

comments.author: small_model
comments.text: All the legacy automakers that haven't fully moved to EV's PROFITABLY will go defacto bankrupt within a few years, there will be some mergers to stay alive but it's game over. Tesla and China companies will own auto, with Tesla capturing most the profit, similar to Apple vs Android phones. Autonomy will further accelerate this.
comments.analysis: While somewhat speculative, the comment reflects a widely held industry view about the dominance of Tesla and Chinese EV makers and the risks for legacy automakers.

comments.author: troyvit
comments.text:
> By shelving EVs, Honda will fall farther behind in two of the biggest shifts sweeping the automotive industry: electric drivetrains [...]

Ugh that sucks

> [...] and software-defined vehicles.

Take my money! I'll suffer with gas for that.
comments.analysis: Concise and accurate summary of the risks Honda faces by not embracing EVs and SDVs, though somewhat emotional in tone.

comments.author: dzonga
comments.text:
I think Japanese automakers by sticking to ICE vehicles have admitted defeat - that they no longer have the engineering prowess to compete.

they dominated in the era of small engines.

with EVs - the Chinese have run away with the stick & sadly no one is catching up.

I wish the Japanese made good EVs - Germans are the only ones besides the Chinese making decent EVs
comments.analysis: Expresses a critical opinion on Japanese automakers' competitiveness in EVs, which aligns with market trends but may be somewhat overstated.

comments.author: jleyank
comments.text:
My cars last 8+ years. My tablets last 3+ years. I’ll pass on a software defined car unless they swap out the whole logic and display unit before the warranty runs out. Otherwise I’ve got dead hardware in the cabin. They did this to the Leaf.

Or assume you have to provide a current model iPad or android tablet to run their software. That would keep the hardware functional if they kept the software working.

And I don’t trust the vendors to try to drive resale by eol’ing the logic/software. They’ll drive everybody to leases to avoid this and battery life concerns.
comments.analysis: Highlights consumer concerns about hardware obsolescence and software-defined vehicles, a valid point regarding longevity and resale value.

comments.author: storus
comments.text: Are they killing their EVs because of vibrations?
comments.analysis: A speculative and unclear comment lacking supporting detail, thus not contributing meaningfully to the discussion.

comments.author: ggm-at-algebras
comments.text:
In Shenzhen for a tech meeting. The streetscape is quieter, despite high traffic levels and I hear not only MORE birdsong, but the birds do more complex songlines.

The air is clean. For sure some of this is because it's a coastal city and has fresh sea breezes, but I've been in other Chinese coastal cities in times past and the air was significantly less clean.

There are social upsides for an almost-all-EV city.

This is an 18m person city. It's not exclusively wealthy people, its just a city with a very high local EV population and it shows.
comments.analysis: Provides anecdotal evidence of environmental benefits from high EV adoption, supporting the positive externalities of EVs.
